parsed.goal: Comprehensive mastery from scratch; seeking an interactive, first-principles alternative to traditional reading.

modules.title: Ground Zero: Rebuilding the Discrete Math Foundation
modules.description: Because you have a 10+ year gap in mathematics, we will start at the very back of the book. We will rebuild your intuition for discrete mathematics from first principles so the proofs ahead feel logical rather than intimidating.

plan.goal: Master the foundational discrete mathematics required to analyze algorithms, including sets, relations, functions, and combinatorics.
sourceRefs: Part VIII Appendix: Mathematical Background (Chapter B, Chapter C)

modules.title: Mathematical Toolkit: Summations, Matrices, and Probability
modules.description: Continuing our foundational build, we delve into the core math tools used to calculate runtime and analyze randomized algorithms.

plan.goal: Comfortably manipulate summations, matrix operations, and discrete probability distributions to prepare for rigorous runtime analysis.
concepts: Bernoulli trials and the binomial distribution
sourceRefs: Part VIII Appendix: Mathematical Background (Chapter A, Chapter C, Chapter D)

modules.title: First Principles of Algorithmic Thinking
modules.description: With your math rebuilt, we open Chapter 1. We'll write our first algorithms, learn how to read CLRS pseudocode, and logically prove that our code works using loop invariants.

plan.goal: Trace pseudocode, implement basic sorting algorithms, and mathematically prove their correctness using loop invariants.
concepts: Definition of an algorithm and computational problems

modules.title: Asymptotic Notation: The Language of Runtime
modules.description: Here we learn how computer scientists talk about speed. We will rigorously define Big-O, Omega, and Theta notation, ensuring you understand the mathematical limits behind them.

plan.goal: Rigorously define, calculate, and prove upper, lower, and tight bounds for algorithm running times.
concepts: Asymptotic limits and limits at infinity
Growth rates of common functions (exponentials, logarithms, factorials)

modules.title: Divide-and-Conquer and Solving Recurrences
modules.description: We explore the core paradigm of breaking big problems into smaller ones. You will learn to implement Merge Sort and use mathematical trees and theorems to solve recursive runtimes.

plan.goal: Design divide-and-conquer algorithms and mathematically solve their recurrence relations using multiple techniques.

modules.title: Probabilistic Analysis and Randomized Algorithms
modules.description: Some of the best algorithms use randomness. We will merge your earlier probability foundation with algorithmic analysis to understand expected runtimes.

plan.goal: Analyze algorithms whose behavior depends on random choices, accurately computing expected running time.
concepts: Using indicator random variables in algorithm analysis

modules.title: Heapsort and Priority Queues
modules.description: Moving into Part II, we build our first complex data structure: the Heap. You'll learn how to implement it, sort with it, and use it as a priority queue.

plan.goal: Implement the heap data structure, use it for in-place sorting, and build a functioning priority queue.
concepts: Building a heap in linear time
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (Chapter 6)

modules.title: Quicksort: The Crown Jewel of Sorting
modules.description: Quicksort is elegantly simple but notoriously tricky to analyze mathematically. We will cover the code deeply, then rigorously prove its average and worst-case runtimes.

plan.goal: Implement Quicksort, understand its partitioning logic, and mathematically prove its average-case and worst-case runtimes.
concepts: Performance of Quicksort (Worst and Best case)
Rigorous analysis of expected running time
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (Chapter 7)

modules.title: Breaking the Sorting Barrier: Linear Time and Order Statistics
modules.description: Can we sort faster than O(n log n)? Yes, under certain conditions. We will explore non-comparison sorts and algorithms to find median elements in linear time.

plan.goal: Prove the lower bound for comparison sorting, implement linear-time sorts, and find order statistics efficiently.
concepts: Decision trees and lower bounds for sorting
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (Chapters 8, 9)

modules.title: Elementary Data Structures and Hashing
modules.description: We shift focus from sorting to storing. You'll build core memory structures from scratch and dive deep into the mathematics of hash collisions and table design.

plan.goal: Implement foundational memory structures and mathematically analyze the performance of various hashing techniques.
concepts: Hash tables and collision resolution via chaining

modules.title: Trees of Order: Binary Search and Red-Black Trees
modules.description: One of the most intensive modules. We cover standard binary search trees, then endure the rigorous, case-by-case logic of Red-Black Trees to guarantee balance.

plan.goal: Implement and analyze Binary Search Trees, and master the complex rotations and invariants required for Red-Black tree balancing.
concepts: Binary Search Tree properties and querying
Maintaining Red-Black balance during insertion (Case analysis)
Maintaining Red-Black balance during deletion (Case analysis)

modules.title: Dynamic Programming: Mastering Optimal Substructure
modules.description: A paradigm shift in algorithm design. We will dissect problems into overlapping subproblems, trading memory for extraordinary speedups via memoization and tabulation.

plan.goal: Recognize optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems to design, mathematically justify, and implement dynamic programming solutions.
concepts: Elements of dynamic programming (Optimal substructure, overlapping subproblems)
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques (Chapter 15)
prerequisites: Binary Search Tree properties and querying

modules.title: Greedy Algorithms and Amortized Analysis
modules.description: Sometimes, taking the immediate best choice leads to the global optimum. We'll prove when this works, and then learn how to average out worst-case costs over time.

plan.goal: Prove the greedy choice property for optimization problems and analyze sequences of operations using amortized techniques.
concepts: Aggregate analysis, accounting method, and potential method
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques (Chapters 16, 17)
prerequisites: Elements of dynamic programming (Optimal substructure, overlapping subproblems)

modules.title: Advanced Data Structures: B-Trees and Heaps
modules.description: We explore structures designed for massive data. From database-backing B-Trees to the theoretical perfection of Fibonacci heaps.

plan.goal: Understand and implement specialized data structures utilized for disk storage and heavy-duty graph algorithms.
concepts: Decreasing a key and bounding maximum degree in Fibonacci Heaps
Path compression and union by rank
prerequisites: Binary Search Tree properties and querying

modules.title: Graph Algorithms Foundation and Minimum Spanning Trees
modules.description: We formally enter the world of Graphs. You'll learn how to represent networks in code, traverse them, and find the cheapest way to connect them.

plan.goal: Represent graphs computationally and implement fundamental traversal and spanning tree algorithms.

modules.title: Shortest Paths: Single-Source and All-Pairs
modules.description: How does GPS work? We dive into the legendary algorithms of Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra, extending them to massive networks.

plan.goal: Implement and mathematically prove the correctness of classical shortest-path algorithms across various graph conditions.
concepts: Relaxation technique and properties of shortest paths
Single-source shortest paths in Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)

modules.title: Maximum Flow Networks
modules.description: Modeling pipelines, traffic grids, and matchings. We explore flow networks and the powerful Max-flow Min-cut theorem.

plan.goal: Model and solve flow network problems using the Ford-Fulkerson method and its optimizations.
concepts: Flow networks and the flow definition

modules.title: Selected Topics: Multithreading, Matrices, and Linear Programming
modules.description: Entering the deep end (Part VII). We transition from sequential to parallel execution, returning to matrix math, and discovering the universal power of Linear Programming.

plan.goal: Transition from sequential to parallel algorithm design and leverage linear programming for complex optimization.
concepts: Multithreaded matrix multiplication and merge sort
Solving systems of linear equations (LUP decomposition)
Linear programming formulations (Standard and Slack forms)

modules.title: Selected Topics: Polynomials, Number Theory, and String Matching
modules.description: We explore algorithms that form the backbone of modern cryptography, signal processing, and text search.

plan.goal: Master specialized computational problems dealing with algebra, cryptography, and string searching algorithms.
concepts: The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and its inverse
Greatest common divisor and modular arithmetic

modules.title: Computational Geometry and NP-Completeness
modules.description: We explore spatial algorithms before hitting the ultimate theoretical wall: NP-Completeness. You will formally learn what computers *cannot* do efficiently.

plan.goal: Solve multi-dimensional geometric problems and formally prove the computational limits of problems using NP-Completeness reductions.
concepts: Finding the closest pair of points
Polynomial time and complexity classes (P vs. NP)
Classic NP-hard reductions (3-CNF, Clique, Vertex-cover, Subset-sum)

modules.title: Approximation Algorithms and Capstone Implementation
modules.description: In our final module, we learn how to handle NP-Complete problems by accepting 'good enough' solutions. We then synthesize the entire cover-to-cover journey into a unified code suite.

plan.goal: Design approximation algorithms for NP-hard problems and synthesize the entire curriculum by finalizing a comprehensive implementation suite.
concepts: Capstone synthesis: Reviewing the dependency graph of all data structures
prerequisites: Classic NP-hard reductions (3-CNF, Clique, Vertex-cover, Subset-sum)

Mathematical Toolkit: Summations, Matrices, and Probability
